segments.s607-g185: ورا is an older fused form of او را meaning 'to him' and functions as the indirect/direct object marker before the noun phrase. خلعت خسروی is a NP in which خلعت ('robe of honour') is modified by خسروی ('royal/kingly') placed after it in the classical/poetic order. ساختند is the simple past third-person plural of ساختن ('to make/put/bestow') and here means 'they bestowed/put (the robe) on him.' The overall word order is object pronoun + object NP + verb (typical older Persian).
segments.s607-g186: سرش را is 'his head' (سر + enclitic ش for 'his') with را as the definite/direct-object marker attached after the possessed noun. به ابر اندر is a poetic prepositional expression: به ('to/towards') + ابر ('cloud') + اندر ('in/within'), together giving 'into/among the clouds' or 'aloud into the clouds'; اندر is an archaic/post-classical preposition. افراختند is the past third-person plural of افراختن ('to raise/erect, to exalt'), so the clause means 'they raised his head (up) into the clouds.' The clause follows SOV tendencies (object phrase before the verb) typical of Ferdowsi's style.
segments.s607-g105: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause meaning “when.” بشنید is past 3rd‑singular “he heard.” Note the poetic V–S order here (beshnid before pir) — the subject پیر (“the old man”) follows the verb. شاد شد is a two‑word predicate where شد is the past copula/‘become’ and shad is the predicative adjective “glad.”
segments.s607-g106: از اندوه دیرینه is a prepositional phrase ‘from (his) long‑standing sorrow,’ with دیرینه qualifying اندوه. آزاد شد is another predicate where شد is the past copula ‘became/was’ and آزاد is the adjective ‘free’ — together ‘was freed’ or ‘became free.’
segments.s607-g107: هم‌انگه is an adverb ‘at that moment.’ سوی خانه شد is a verbal phrase meaning ‘turned/went toward the house,’ with شد functioning as the intransitive verbal element; classical/poetic order places the verb before its subject, so مرد پیر (the old man) follows the verb and is its agent.
segments.s607-g108: بیاورد is past 3rd‑singular ‘he brought’ and takes مردم as its object; سوی آبگیر is a directional prepositional phrase ‘toward the pond.’ The agent (مرد پیر) is understood from the previous clause, so the verb appears here before the object for poetic compactness.
segments.s607-g109: زمین را is the definite object (marked by را). به آباد کردن is an infinitival phrase ‘to make prosperous/for prospering’ (آباد کردن = ‘to cultivate/make prosperous’) introduced by به; گرفت (lit. ‘took’) is used idiomatically with an infinitive to mean ‘took up/began’ — so ‘he began to make the land prosperous.’
segments.s607-g110: همه مرزها را is the object ‘all the borders/frontiers’ (را marks definiteness). سپردن is the infinitive ‘to entrust/hand over’ and گرفت again means ‘took up/began’ — together ‘he began entrusting all the frontiers.’ The beneficiary (to whom they were entrusted) is omitted, typical of concise epic style.
segments.s607-g111: ز here is the poetic preposition ‘from’ (ز همسایگان = ‘from the neighbours’). گاو و خر are the objects requested, coordinated by و. خواستند is past 3rd‑plural ‘they asked/wished for’ (i.e., they requested/received cattle).
segments.s607-g112: همه دشت is the noun phrase ‘the whole/plain’ (همه + noun = entire X). یکسر is an adverb ‘entirely/wholly,’ and بیاراستند is past 3rd‑plural ‘they adorned/arranged,’ so the clause means ‘they entirely adorned the whole plain.’ The verb at the end is the regular Persian past plural form.
segments.s607-g9: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause ('when'). خورشید تابان is a noun (خورشید) plus an attributive adjective (تابان, a poetic epithet 'shining') that follows the noun. به گنبد رسید is a prepositional phrase (به + گنبد) followed by the past tense verb رسید (3rd singular) meaning 'reached/arrived at'.
segments.s607-g10: به جایی functions adverbially ('to/at a place' = 'anywhere'); پی here means 'track/trace' (a poetic use for signs or traces), followed by the coordinated nouns گور و آهو ('grave and gazelle'). ندید is the past negative verb (3rd singular) 'did not see', taking the preceding phrase as its object. The whole segment completes the main clause begun by چو…رسید.
segments.s607-g11: چو again introduces 'when'. خورشید تابان is the same nominal phrase as before. درم ساز is a compound predicative (درم 'dirham/coin' + ساز 'maker/means' in a poetic/metaphoric sense) serving as the complement of گشت, which is the past-tense copular/verb 'became/turned'. This is a figurative/metaphorical expression typical of epic poetry.
segments.s607-g12: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. نخچیرگه is a compound noun 'hunting-ground'. تنگدل is an adjectival epithet 'narrow‑hearted / melancholic' describing the subject (implicit 'he'), and بازگشت is the past verb 'returned' (here 3rd singular). Word order is PP (ز نخچیرگه) + epithet + verb, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s607-g13: به پیش اندر آمد is a multi-word verbal phrase meaning 'came forward / entered before them' (به پیش = to the front; اندر آمد = entered/came in). یکی is an indefinite 'one / a certain', and سبز جای is a noun phrase with سبز 'green' qualifying جای 'place/position'—a compact poetic description (literally 'a certain green-place' or 'a green-positioned one'). The phrase places an entity before the spectators.
segments.s607-g14: بسی is an intensifier 'many / much' qualifying the following nouns; اندرو (an older form of اندر او/در او) means 'in it/within', linking back to 'سبز جای'. مردم و چارپای is a coordination 'people and quadrupeds/beasts'. There is no main verb here because this is a nominal clause describing what the green-place contained.
segments.s607-g15: ازان is a poetic locative 'from that / from there'. فراوان is an adverb 'in great numbers / plentifully'. به راه آمدند is a verbal phrase 'came onto the road' or 'came out onto the way' with آمدند as the past plural 'they came'. The clause describes a mass movement of people from that place.
segments.s607-g16: نظاره here functions as 'spectators' or 'things to be seen' (a poetic noun), followed by the locative phrase به پیش سپاه 'before the army / in front of the host'. آمدند is again the past plural verb 'they came/appeared'. The clause states that onlookers presented themselves before the army.
segments.s607-g57: شده is a perfect/past participle or resultative 'become' used predicatively (poetic order: verb before its complements). دشت ویران is noun + adjective (the plain, ruined). و introduces a parallel noun phrase ویران سرای (literally 'ruined-house/dwelling'); note سرای here should mean 'dwelling/house' (poetic). The whole clause states a resulting state: 'the plain became ruined and the dwellings desolate.'
segments.s607-g58: رمیده is a past participle/adjective from رَمیدن meaning 'fled' or 'scattered' and here describes the subject (people and beasts) as having fled. ازو is the archaic fused form of از او 'from it/him/her' and marks source. مردم و چارپای are the coordinated nouns 'people and quadrupeds/animals.' The clause therefore reads 'people and animals had fled from it.'
segments.s607-g59: چو is the archaic conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause. یک سال بگذشت uses the past verb بگذشت ('passed') to mean 'a year passed' (simple past narrative). و آمد بهار adds a second past action 'and spring came' — standard past-sequence coordination with و.
segments.s607-g60: بران is an adverbial/archaic locative 'on that/thereon' pointing back to the place; ره 'way' follows it (adverb + noun). به نخچیر is a prepositional phrase 'for/unto hunting' (نخچیر = game/hunt). شد here is the past of شدن used in an intransitive, narrative sense 'set out / went' and شهریار is the subject 'the king' placed after the verb for poetic word order.
segments.s607-g61: بران ('thereon' / 'to that place') again introduces location. جای آباد خرم is a sequence of nouns/adjectives: جای 'place', آباد 'prosperous/inhabited', خرم 'joyous/lush' — a nominal phrase 'that prosperous, cheerful place'. رسید is the past verb 'arrived/reached', completing the clause.
segments.s607-g62: نگه کرد is the light-verb construction meaning 'he looked/gazed' (نگه + کرد, where کرد is the common auxiliary/lexical verb). و coordinates the next clause. بر جای بر ده ندید is elliptical Poetic word order: بر جای 'on that spot' + بر ده (probably 'on the village' or 'in the hamlet') + ندید 'he did not see' (ندید is past negative of دیدن). The double بر are prepositional uses (on/upon) and ندید negates the seeing — 'he looked and saw no one there or in the village.'
segments.s607-g63: درختان همه خشک is noun + quantifier + adjective: 'all the trees [were] dry' (ellipsis of copula is normal in Persian poetry). و links to ویران‌سرای, a compound meaning 'ruined-dwelling(s)' — here the clause lists parallel desolations: trees dried and houses ruined.
segments.s607-g64: همه مرز 'the whole frontier/region' is the subject. بی‌مردم is an adjectival compound meaning 'devoid of people / deserted.' و چارپای adds 'and quadrupeds/animals.' The clause therefore says the entire border/region was without people or animals; word order is typical poetic (subject phrase then predicate adjectives).
segments.s607-g137: This is a verbless, poetic clause where quality/state is predicated: 'همه' (all/every) is a universal determiner, followed by the noun 'راغ' (here 'desire/longing') and then 'آب' (water), joined by 'و' (and) to a parallel nominal phrase 'همه دشت جوی' (literally 'every plain [is] a stream'). The copula 'is/was' is omitted as is common in Persian poetry, so the two noun phrases are understood as equational descriptions.
segments.s607-g138: 'همه ده پر از مردم خوب‌روی' is another nominal clause: 'همه' (every/all) + 'ده' (village) where the adjective phrase 'پر از مردم خوب‌روی' ('full of good‑faced people') follows. 'پر از' is a fixed adjectival construction meaning 'full of', with 'از' as the governed preposition introducing the content ('مردم خوب‌روی'). Word order is typical: determiner + head noun + adjective phrase.
segments.s607-g139: 'پراگنده' (scattered) works as a predicate adjective modifying the following locative phrase. 'بر کوه و دشتش' is a prepositional phrase ('on/upon the mountain and his plain'); note the possessive suffix on 'دشتش' ('his plain'). The final noun 'بره' ('lamb') is the subject/affected thing — in poetic word order the adjective and locative precede the nominal it describes.
segments.s607-g140: 'بهشتی شده' is a compound predicate where 'شده' is the perfect/passive participle 'has become' and 'بهشتی' is the adjective 'heavenly' — literally 'has become heavenly'. 'بوم او' ('his bōm/land') is the subject or affected noun, and 'یکسره' is an adverb meaning 'entirely/altogether' placed after the noun for emphasis. Word order here is typical Persian: predicate after subject-phrase, but poetry allows permutations.
segments.s607-g141: 'به موبد چنین گفت' is a reporting clause: 'به' (to) + 'موبد' (the mōbed/priest) marks the indirect object/recipient; 'چنین گفت' ('said thus') introduces the quoted or reported speech. 'کای روزبه' is a vocative: 'کای' = 'O' addressing 'روزبه' (a name). The verb of saying is packaged with the adverb 'چنین' for emphasis.
segments.s607-g142: This is the content of the address. 'چه کردی' is the question 'what did you do/what have you done' (perfective past used as a direct question). The conjunction 'که' introduces the result/clause 'that …' explaining the consequence: 'ویران بد این خوب ده' — word order here places the adjectives before the head noun: 'ویران' (ruined) + 'بد' (ill/bad) describe 'این خوب ده' ('this good village'), so literally 'that ruined bad this good‑village' → 'that has ruined this fine village.'
segments.s607-g143: 'پراگنده زو' uses the adjective 'پراگنده' (scattered/widespread) followed by the archaic/preclassical preposition 'زو' (poetic form of 'از', 'from') marking source. The coordinated noun phrase 'مردم و چارپای' ('people and quadrupeds') is the thing scattered. Word order again is adjective + source + coordinated nouns, typical of poetic inversion.
segments.s607-g144: 'چه دادی' is the interrogative 'what did you give?' with 'دادی' the 2nd‑person singular past of 'دادن' (to give). The conjunction 'که' introduces the consequent clause 'آباد کردند جای' — here 'آباد' (prosperous/inhabited) and the verb 'کردند' (they made/did) combine to mean 'they made (it) prosperous', with 'جای' (place) as the object. The sequence asks for the cause: 'what did you do/give so that they made the place prosper?'
segments.s607-g113: خود و مرزداران is a coordinated subject ('they themselves and the frontier‑guards'); بکوشید is a past tense verb (3rd‑pl.) meaning 'strove/tried' with an archaic poetic prefix ب‑; سخت is an adverbial intensifier placed after the verb meaning 'hard/firmly'.
segments.s607-g114: بکشتند is the past verb meaning 'they planted / cultivated' here (not 'killed'); هرجای is an adverbial 'in every place/wherever'; چندی quantifies the following noun as 'a few/several'; درخت is the direct object 'trees' — word order reflects poetic inversion but reads 'they planted several trees wherever'.
segments.s607-g115: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; یک برزن is the noun phrase 'one plain/field'; نیک آباد is a compound adjective 'well/beautifully inhabited, prosperous'; شد is the past copular/auxiliary 'became' — together: 'when one plain became prosperous'.
segments.s607-g116: دل هرک دید literally 'the heart of whoever saw' — هرک که دید is a relative idea 'whoever saw (it)' with the relative particle implied; اندران 'in/therein' specifies the place; شاد شد is 'became joyful' (شاد = happy, شد = became). The word order places 'دل' (heart) up front for emphasis: 'the heart of each viewer rejoiced.'
segments.s607-g117: ازان جای = 'from that place' (ازان is the poetic form of 'from that'); هرکس که بگریختی is a relative clause 'whoever fled.' The verb form بگریختی here is an archaic/poetic past form used in the relative clause 'whoever fled.'
segments.s607-g118: به مژگان = 'onto/onto the eyelashes' (به marking the target); همی is a habitual/continuous particle here (indicating repeated or ongoing action); خون فرو ریختی = 'blood poured down' — فرو is the preverb 'down(ward)' combined with ریختی (archaic past of 'to pour/fall'), producing 'blood streamed/poured down.'
segments.s607-g119: چو is 'when'; آگاهی آمد = 'news/information came' (آگاهی as 'information/news' is the grammatical subject and آمد is the past 'came'); ز آباد جای = 'from the prosperous place' with poetic z (ز) = از 'from' placed before the noun phrase آباد جای.
segments.s607-g120: هم از = 'also about/from'; رنج این پیر سر کدخدای = 'the suffering of this old head (کدخدای = village‑headman)'; سر here reads as 'chief/head' modifying کدخدای, so the phrase means 'the suffering of this old village‑chief.' The whole segment states that news also came concerning that elder's hardship.
segments.s607-g25: کنام here is a locative/relational noun meaning 'the bosom/abode of' placed before a series of nouns; the three nouns دد و دام و نخچیر are simply coordinated by و. The final باد is the archaic/poetic optative particle ("may it be..."), so the whole segment expresses a wish: 'may (this place) be the abode of X'.
segments.s607-g26: به introduces the locative phrase: 'into/in the stream' (به + جوی اندرون آب = 'in/into the stream's inner water'). اندرون specifies 'inside', and the simile چون قیر 'like tar' follows. The clause ends again with the optative باد ('may it be'), so syntactically this is a second wished state applying to the stream-water.
segments.s607-g27: بدانست is a past tense verb meaning 'he knew/realized'; the subject موبد follows it (verb–subject order is common in classical Persian). The conjunction/particle که introduces the subordinate clause 'that the king's command...', so this segment gives the main verb plus the beginning of its complement.
segments.s607-g28: This continues the subordinate clause and then gives the following action: چه بود 'what (it) was' completes the complement to بدانست. اندران functions adverbially ('therefrom/thereupon' or 'from there'), سوی ده شد = 'turned/set off toward the village' with سوی marking direction and شد used as the simple past/motion verb 'became/turned/got to'. ز راه is a prepositional phrase 'from the road' (ز = poetic 'from').
segments.s607-g29: بدیشان ('to them') is an indirect-object/dative phrase; چنین 'thus' modifies گفت 'said' (here the past-tense verb 'گفت' — 'he said') and the complementizer ک + این سبزجای begins the reported content. Word order is indirect object + manner + verb + complementizer + demonstrative-noun phrase.
segments.s607-g30: پر از is the adjectival phrase 'full of': پر ('full') is followed by the preposition از ('of') and a coordinated list خانه و مردم و چارپای. The conjunction و links the items; چارپای is a general term for four-footed animals/beasts. This whole noun phrase is the content of what was said.
segments.s607-g31: خوش‌آمد here functions as a predicative/verbal idea 'it was pleasing / welcomed' and, with the following noun phrase, yields the natural reading 'the king welcomed Bahram.' The object marker را attached to بهرام marks Bahram as the definite object. Classical word order allows the predicate before the subject (خوش‌آمد ... شهنشاه).
segments.s607-g32: یکی here functions as an indefinite/indefinite-adverbial ('one/again/somewhat') and تازه کرد is 'made fresh/renewed' where کرد is the causative/active verb 'to make/do' that combines with the adjective تازه. اندرین کام را is the object phrase 'this desire' (اندرین = 'in these/this'), with را marking it definite. Together: 'he made/renewed this desire.'
segments.s607-g17: This is a past‑tense copular clause: subject (جهاندار, 'the sovereign') + two adjectival predicates in a coordinate construction (پرخشم 'wrathful' and پرتاب 'impetuous/ready to fling/impetuous') joined by و, followed by the past copula بود 'was'. The order is subject–predicates–copula, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s607-g18: همی خواست is an imperfective/past habitual construction meaning 'he desired/was intending' (همی marking continuous aspect + خواست past of 'want'). کاید is the object 'a plot/contrivance', and بدان ده فرود functions as the goal/location: ب (to/into) + دان (here part of the locative notion 'on that') + فرود 'descent/coming down' — together 'to descend on that village'. The clause expresses purpose: 'he intended a plot to descend upon that village.'
segments.s607-g19: نکردند is the negative past plural 'they did not do' (here 'did not utter/praise'); زِ ایشان 'of/from them' specifies the group; کسی with a negated verb yields 'no one' — آفرین is the exclamation 'bravo' or 'praise'. The whole means 'no one among them cried “bravo.”'
segments.s607-g20: This is reported speech framed by subject 'تو' 'you' + (implied) verb 'گفتی' 'you said', followed by a verb phrase 'ببست آن خران را زمین'. ببست here carries the meaning 'bind/tie' (perfect/past form), آن خران را زمین is the direct object with را marking specificity: 'those donkeys to the ground.' Word order is subject–verb (said) then quoted/command clause.
segments.s607-g21: ازان مردمان is a prepositional/agentive phrase 'because of those people' or 'from those people'; تنگدل is an adjective 'narrow‑hearted/sorrowful' and گشت is the past intransitive 'became/turned' with شاه 'the king' as complement. The word order places the cause phrase up front (ازان مردمان) then the resulting state of the king.
segments.s607-g22: به خوبی نکرد is a negated light‑verb construction: به خوبی 'with kindness' + نکرد 'did not do' = 'did not do good / did not treat kindly'. اندر ایشان نگاه 'toward them a look' (اندر = 'upon/in/among', ایشان = 'them', نگاه = 'gaze/regard') completes the clause: 'he did not look upon them kindly.'
segments.s607-g23: به موبد 'to the mobed' marks the addressee. چنین گفت is a formulaic 'thus he said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = 'he said'). کاین سبز جای is a nominal phrase 'that/this green place' (کاین = 'this/that', سبز = 'green/verdant', جای = 'place'). The clause introduces reported speech.
segments.s607-g24: پر از is the idiom 'full of' (پر 'full' + از 'of'), followed by a coordinated list of contents: خانه 'houses', مردم 'people', چارپای 'quadrupeds/beasts of burden'. The list is connected by و 'and'. The whole is the predicate of the reported clause: 'is full of houses, people and beasts of burden.'
segments.s607-g121: یکایک is an adverb meaning 'one by one' or 'each in turn' and modifies the following clause. سوی + (ده) expresses direction 'toward the village'; نهادند is the past plural of نهادن 'to place/set' used here with روی ('face') — نهادند روی = 'set (their) face' i.e. 'turned (their) faces'. The sequence places the adverbial and directional phrase before the verb, as is common in classical Persian.
segments.s607-g122: به هر برزن is an adverbial phrase 'in/at every barzan (open place or village square)'; آباد کردند is a causative/perfective construction 'they made (it) prosperous/they built/settled it' (کردند with the adjective آباد). جوی (placed after the verb) is the thing they made/dug — 'a stream/channel'. Word order here places the adverbial front, verb before the object (جوی) which is normal in poetic prose.
segments.s607-g123: A simple enumerative noun phrase listing animals; همان here emphasizes 'those same' or 'the very'. The nouns are coordinated by و and form the (collective) subject of the next clause.
segments.s607-g124: یکایک again 'one by one / each in turn' modifies the verb. برافزود is a past tense (3rd person) of برافزودن meaning 'increased, grew, added' — here intransitive 'grew (in number)'. بر is the preposition 'on/upon' (here introducing what follows), and کشتمند is a nominal form; in this line it functions as the complement introduced by بر. (Note: کشتمند is lexically 'murderer' or 'deadly' in the supplied senses, but the precise semantic role in this context is archaic/poetic and could be idiomatic — grammatically it is a noun governed by بر.)
segments.s607-g125: درختی is an indefinite object 'a tree' (fronted for emphasis). به هر جای هرکس is a free relative/adverbial: به هر جای = 'in/at any place' and هرکس = 'whoever / anyone' (the agent). بکشت is an archaic past form (from the root shown as بک) meaning 'planted' or 'did plant'; classical Persian often places the verb at the clause end, as here.
segments.s607-g126: شد is the past of شدن 'to become' and introduces the result clause. آن جای ویران = 'that ruined place' (modifier + head noun). چو is the comparative/simile 'as/like', and خرم بهشت 'a lush/joyful paradise' is the simile's standard; word order places the verb before the predicative phrase for poetic effect.
segments.s607-g127: به سالی سه دیگر is a temporal phrase 'in three more years' (به سالی + number + دیگر = within/after three further years). بیاراست is past tense of بیاراستن 'to adorn/arrange' and here means 'was adorned' or 'they adorned (it)'; ده (village) is the affected place (omitted from the listed words but present in the line). The phrase expresses that the village gained further embellishment over that time.
segments.s607-g128: برآمد is 'arose/ensued' or 'resulted'. ز ورزش is a causal phrase 'from/through exertion or industry' (ز = 'from', ورزش = 'practice/labor'). همه کام مه literally 'all (همه) desire(s) (کام) of مه' — مه here is the noun 'mah' (literally 'moon') used poetically (often as an epithet); grammatically the three words form a genitive-like unit 'all the desires of Mah'. The clause says that by effort all those desires were realized. (Note: مه is poetically loaded and may refer metaphorically to a person or lofty ideal.)
segments.s607-g177: خرد شاه is a genitive noun phrase ("the wisdom of the king") where the modifier (خرد) precedes the possessor (شاه). باید is a modal/auxiliary meaning "must/should" and introduces a predicate nominal (زبان پهلوان) — the copula "to be" is omitted (poetic ellipsis), so the phrase reads literally "the king's wisdom must be the champion's tongue/speech."
segments.s607-g178: چو here introduces a conditional/temporal clause ("if/when"); خواهی is 2nd-person present "you want/wish," and که introduces the subordinate clause "that…". ماند is a poetic form of the present-subjunctive/optative "remain" (the prefix ب- is often dropped in poetry), with روان ("soul/spirit") as the semantic subject and بی‌رنج ("free from pain/untroubled") as the predicative description.
segments.s607-g179: دل شاه is another genitive phrase "the king's heart." تا جاودان is an adverbial phrase meaning "until/for eternity" (i.e. "forever"). شاد is the predicative adjective "happy/glad" and باد is the optative particle "may (it be)" placed after the predicate, a common poetic construction to express wishes.
segments.s607-g180: ز is the poetic preposition "from/of," introducing what the subject should be free of (کژی و ویرانی = "crookedness and ruin"). آباد باد is again predicate + optative "may (it) be prosperous/inhabited," with the subject left implicit (referring back to the king's realm/condition). The order (preposition phrase before predicate) and omission of an explicit subject are typical in verse.
segments.s607-g181: چو بشنید forms a temporal subordinate clause "when he heard" (چو = "when"). شاه is the subject "the king." این سخن is the object "this speech/these words." گفت is the verb "said," but here it is followed by زه, an interjection introducing his utterance; in this segment the verb and the introductory interjection stand before the direct speech. (Note: the lemma senses list did not include the verbal sense of گفت, so that sense is marked separately.)
segments.s607-g182: سزاوار تاجی is a predicate phrase meaning "worthy/deserving of a crown" (سزاوار + noun in the indefinite/qualifying form تاجی). تو‌ای روزبه is a vocative: تو‌ای is the emphatic/poetic address "you—O," followed by the name روزبه. The predicate precedes the vocative in the poetic order, giving the sense "You, Rouzbeh, are deserving of a crown."
segments.s607-g183: ببخشید is a past-tense/perfect verb here meaning "he bestowed/gave" (in poetry the same form can read as past). The noun phrase یک بدره دینار زرد is the direct object: یک (one) + بدره (used poetically as a simile "like the full moon" / bright) modifying دینار (dinar/coin) and زرد ("yellow/golden") as a final color adjective; the modifiers precede the head noun in a compact poetic listing.
segments.s607-g184: بران is a prepositional/relational particle here meaning "to/for that" (linking back to the beneficiary of the gift). پرهنر is an adjectival compound "full of skill/cultured" placed before the head noun مرد (a heroic/valiant man). بیننده appears as an attributive participle/adjective "perceiving/observant/beholding" between the two مردs; the final مرد is the head noun. Overall, the phrase is a piled set of epithets: "to that cultured, discerning man, the hero."
segments.s607-g89: Literal word order: determiner + noun + locative + predicate. همه modifies کار ('the whole/entire affair'); اینجا is the locative 'here'; پر از is the idiom 'full of' (پر + از + noun); تیرگیست is a nominal predicate formed with تیرگی ('darkness/gloom') plus the copula -ست. The segment as a unit means 'the whole situation here is full of darkness.'
segments.s607-g90: چنان شد is a verbal phrase 'it became/it came to be so'; که introduces a result clause. بر ما is a dative/benefactive/locative sense 'upon us' (here 'for us'); بباید is the modal 'it is necessary / one must' (present imperfective modal), and گریست is the infinitive/masdar-like form 'to weep' used with the modal. The clause means 'so it became that we must weep.'
segments.s607-g91: ازین is a fused prepositional phrase 'from/at this' (از + این) introducing cause or source; گفته is a noun here 'that utterance/statement'; پردرد شد is a compound predicate 'became pained/sorrowful' (پردرد = full of pain, شد = became). Word order is cause/source → phrase → result: 'From this statement he became sorrowful.'
segments.s607-g92: بپرسید is the past form 'he asked' (بَـ- prefix + پرسید). و links the clauses; گفت here functions as 'he said' introducing reported speech. از شما is 'of/among you' (asking who among you); کیست is composed of the interrogative کی 'who' plus the copula ست 'is'; مه in this context is being used as a substantive meaning 'the great / important one' (i.e. 'the chief'). The whole clause: 'he asked and said, "Who among you is the great one?"'
segments.s607-g93: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed phrasing 'gave the following answer' (چنین = 'thus/so', داد = 'gave', پاسخ = 'answer'). که introduces reported content; مهتر بود is a simple past copular clause 'he/it was the chief' (مهتر = 'chief/elder', بود = 'was').
segments.s607-g94: به جایی is 'to/at a place' (به + جایی); که introduces a relative clause 'where/that'; تخم گیا is a compound 'seed(s) of plants' (تخم = 'seed', گیا = 'plant/vegetation'); بر بود is literally 'was upon' (بر + بود) with the meaning 'lay/was located'. Together: 'at the place where the seeds of plants were (lay).'
segments.s607-g95: بدو is the prepositional phrase 'to him' (به + او) here meaning 'to that man'; روزبه is the speaker's name; گفت functions as 'said'; مهتر تو باش is an imperative clause with مهتر 'chief' as vocative/predicated title, تو 'you', and باش the imperative 'be' — 'You be the chief.'
segments.s607-g96: بدین جای ویران is a locative phrase 'over/at this ruined place' (بدین = به + این 'to/at this', جای = 'place', ویران = 'ruined/desolate'). The second half به سر بر تو باش is an idiomatic imperative: به سر بر تو باش = 'may the head/be in charge be upon you' → 'preside; let the leadership be yours' (سر = 'head', بر = 'on/upon', تو = 'you', باش = imperative 'be'). Overall: 'Be the head over this ruined place.'
segments.s607-g49: بماندند is absent here; this clause uses the past tense verb برآمیختند (3rd pl. past) before its subject in a poetic inversion: همه (‘all/everyone’) + یک به دیگر (‘one to another’) form the subject phrase meaning ‘all with one another’ or ‘everyone among themselves’. The preposition به here (in the idiom یک به دیگر) marks the direction/reciprocity 'to/towards', so the whole clause reads 'all mingled with one another.'
segments.s607-g50: به (preposition) + هرجای (‘wherever/every place’) introduces the location/extent; بی‌راه is an adjectival qualifier modifying the locus (literally ‘wrong/off the road’), and خون ریختند is a past action 'they poured/spilled blood'. Together the clause means 'they shed blood in every (wrong/unguided) place' or more naturally 'blood flowed everywhere off the road.'
segments.s607-g51: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'; برخاست is the simple past 'rose/arose' (3rd sg.), and the noun رستخیز ('uprising') is the subject that 'rose'. زان روستا is a prepositional phrase 'from that village' (زان = 'from that/of that'). The word order is inverted for poetic effect: 'When the uprising rose from that village...'.
segments.s607-g52: گرفتند (past, 3rd pl.) + گریز (noun 'flight') forms a light-verb construction 'they took flight' where گرفتند = 'they took'. ناگاه is an adverb 'suddenly' modifying the verb, and ازان ده ('from that village') gives the source of the flight. The clause reads 'they suddenly took flight from that village.'
segments.s607-g53: بماندند is the past tense (3rd pl.) 'they remained/ were left', placed before its nominal subject پیران ('the elders') by poetic inversion. ابی پای is an adjectival phrase where ابی ('blue') qualifies پای ('foot'), and the coordinating و پر attaches a second descriptive word ('پر'), forming a concise, image-rich description appended to 'pیران'.
segments.s607-g54: بشد here is the past/imperfective of شدن meaning 'it became' or 'it came to pass' in narrative; آلت is the head noun 'instrument/implement' and is followed by a coordinated list (ورزش و ساز و بر) which functions as the complement governed by آلت — 'instruments of ورزش, ساز and بر'. The clause thus states that these things 'became the implements/tools' (i.e., were used as instruments) of exercise, music, etc.
segments.s607-g55: همه ده is a noun phrase 'the whole village' (همه = 'entire/whole' modifying ده = 'village'), followed by the verbal phrase به ویرانی آورد روی. آورد (past of آوردن) 'brought' or 'caused', with به ویرانی 'to destruction', and روی here functions adverbially/locatively ('upon/onto' or idiomatically 'to the state of'). The clause means 'they brought the whole village to ruin.'
segments.s607-g56: درختان is the subject 'the trees'; شده (past participle/verb 'became') predicates a resulting state, and خشک 'dry' gives the first resultant adjective. The coordinate و بی‌آب جوی attaches a second clause-like nominal phrase: بی‌آب modifies جوی ('the stream'), so the sense is 'the trees became dry and the stream became waterless.' The single شد(ه) covers both predicates by position.
segments.s607-g41: This is a coordinated nominal phrase: 'زن و کودک و مرد' lists nouns joined by و (and). جمله is a postpositive quantifier meaning 'all, entire' that attaches to the preceding noun phrase. مهید is an archaic/poetic adjective or clitic modifying the whole phrase; its exact meaning is obscure here, so it is best read as an emphatic/old-fashioned marker ('all of them' or an epithet).
segments.s607-g42: یکایک 'one by one' is an adverbial quantifier placed before همه 'all/everyone' for emphasis; both together stress individual handing-over. کدخدای is the indirect object (the recipient). دهید is a 2nd-person plural (or polite) imperative / hortative form of دادن 'to give' — a command 'give/hand over'.
segments.s607-g43: خروشی 'a cry/uproar' is the subject; برآمد is a past tense verb 'arose/issued forth'. ز here is the poetic preposition 'from' and links the source to the verb; پرمایه qualifies ده (understood 'village') and means 'abundant' or 'well-provisioned', so 'from the prosperous village.'
segments.s607-g44: This clause gives the reason or cause: ز شادی 'from/with joy' and که introduces the explanatory clause ('because'/'for that'), followed by گشتند 'they became' (past tense, 3rd plural) and همواره 'always/continually' modifying the verb. مه is an archaic particle/clitic similar to previous occurrences (emphatic or dialectal), not altering tense but adding an old-fashioned emphatic flavor.
segments.s607-g45: زن و مرد is another coordinated noun phrase. ازان پس 'after that' is a temporal phrase placed before the main clause. یکی شد 'became one' uses شد (past) as a copular/auxiliary verb meaning 'became / were made one'. به رای is a prepositional phrase 'in opinion / in counsel' indicating how they became one (i.e. united in opinion).
segments.s607-g46: This is a short coordinated group naming the parties: پرستار 'caregiver/caretaker' and مزدور 'mercenary/hired man'. با introduces accompaniment 'with' and کدخدای 'the village headman' is the noun governed by با — i.e. these people were united together with the headman.
segments.s607-g47: چو is the archaic/poetic conjunction 'when'. ناباک here describes the moral state of the referred person; although the lemma list gives a positive gloss, the context requires reading it as 'shameless/degenerate' (an older or ironic sense). شد is past 'became', and مرد برنا is the noun phrase 'the young/robust man' (برنا = youthful/vigorous). به ده gives the location 'in/at the village' (به + ده).
segments.s607-g48: This is the action clause: بریدند is a past tense, 3rd-person plural verb 'they cut' (they severed). ناگه 'suddenly' is an adverb modifying the verb. سر مرد 'the man's head' is the direct object (noun + possessor); مه again is the archaic/emphatic particle seen elsewhere and does not affect core syntax.
segments.s607-g81: بیامد is a past 3rd‑singular verb 'came' placed before its subject (common V‑S order in Persian). یکی here is an indefinite 'a certain/one' introducing the subject, and بی‌خرد modifies موبدی (adjective before noun) to give 'a foolish mobad.' The whole segment reads literally 'came a certain foolish mobad.'
segments.s607-g82: ازان is a deictic 'of those/from those' linking this man to a named group; نامداران is a plural attributive 'the renowned ones.' بی‌بر is a compound adjective 'without offspring/childless' (بی‑ prefix = without, بر = offspring), and بدی is a noun/epithet 'badness/evil' used adjectivally here to describe him. Together they form a descriptive tag: 'one of those renowned, childless, wicked ones.'
segments.s607-g83: به ما گفت is an indirect speech frame: به + object 'to us' + گفت (past 'said'). یکسر is an adverb 'utterly/entirely' modifying the following predicate. همه مهترید is the predication 'you are all eminent/great' (مهترید is a 2nd‑plural/formal predicate).
segments.s607-g84: نگر is an imperative 'look!/see!' introducing a purpose/result clause. تا here means 'so that / in order that / lest' and introduces the subordinate negative clause. کسی را is the definite/indefinite direct object (را marks it), به کس is a comparative phrase 'by/against any other person,' and نشمرید is a negative present subjunctive/prohibitive 'do not count/consider' (literary usage: ن‑ + شمر stem). The clause means 'see that you do not compare/esteem anyone by another.'
segments.s607-g85: بگفت is a past form 'he said/said he' (V precedes what follows). این و این is an idiomatic repetition 'this and that' meaning various remarks; پرآشوب is an adjective 'tumultuous/filled with unrest' and گشت is the past verb 'became/turned.' So the segment says he spoke various things and then 'it became tumultuous.'
segments.s607-g86: پر از ... گشت is a standard construction 'became full of ...' where پر (full) + از (of) introduces a list of nouns. The nouns غارت, کشتن, چوب are coordinated by و and name what filled the place: 'plunder, killing, and clubs/sticks' (i.e., violence). گشت again is 'became.'
segments.s607-g87: که here introduces a wish or clause linked to what was said; یزدان is 'God' and ورا 'to him' (dative). یار is predicate 'friend/ally.' به اندازه is an adverbial phrase 'to the extent/measure' and باد at the end is the optative particle 'may it be' (classical Persian wish). The clause therefore expresses a wish: 'May God be his ally to the full.'
segments.s607-g88: This is a short curse/wish clause listing nouns (غم و مرگ و سختی = 'sorrow and death and hardship'). برو تازه باد uses برو (literally 'go' or 'be gone/come') together with تازه ('anew/fresh') plus the optative باد 'may it be,' yielding 'may [these] be newly upon him' — i.e. 'may sorrow, death, and hardship be renewed upon him.' The sequence is idiomatic/political‑poetic for a wished misfortune.
segments.s607-g73: This clause is a compact past action in classical/poetic Persian. ز برزن ('from the square') uses the preposition ز before a place; همی is the archaic/progressive particle marking continuous or iterative action in past narration; سوی + noun marks direction 'toward'; شتافت is the past tense verb 'hastened/ran' and takes the preceding phrase as its complement. Word order is prepositional phrase → directional → verb, typical of Ferdowsi's verse.
segments.s607-g74: به فرجام introduces the result or terminal point 'to/at the end'; بیکار پیری is a nominal phrase meaning 'the idleness/uselessness of old age' (بیکار modifies پیری); بیافت is a past form meaning 'to fall into' or 'befall' (here 'fell into'). The clause therefore expresses an eventual state he came to, using به + noun + verb to mark the end/result.
segments.s607-g75: فرود آمد is a two‑word past construction meaning 'he descended / alighted' (فرود as alighting + آمد as past of 'come'). از باره here means 'on that occasion' (literally 'from the instance') and introduces what he did then; بنواختش is a past transitive verb with the 3rd‑person object suffix -ش 'him/it' meaning 'he struck him' (بـ- prefix + نواخت, poetic past form).
segments.s607-g76: بر خویش نزدیک is a locative phrase 'near himself' where خویش is expressed as خوی + possessive ش (his own). بنشاختش is a causative/past form meaning 'he set/placed him (to sit)' with the object suffix -ش. The clause thus means 'he seated him near himself'; note the verb follows the locative, a common poetic order.
segments.s607-g77: بدو گفت is a short sequence 'he said to him' where بدو is the prepositional pronoun 'to him' and گفت is the past tense verb 'said' (here used as a verb rather than the noun senses sometimes listed). کای is the vocative particle 'O' and خواجه سالخورد is the addressed person 'aged master' (خواجه noun + سالخورد adjective after it).
segments.s607-g78: This is an interrogative/exclamatory nominal clause compressed by poetic word order: چنین ('such/so') modifies جای آباد ('a prosperous place'), then ویران ('ruined') describes its state; که introduces the clause whose verb follows, and کرد is the past 'made/did'. Together the phrase asks 'How did you make such a prosperous place desolate?' with the subject implied or supplied in the surrounding speech.
segments.s607-g79: چنین داد پاسخ is a formulaic phrase 'thus he gave answer' — چنین ('thus') + داد ('gave', past of 'to give') + پاسخ ('answer'). The particle که introduces the reported content clause that follows (equivalent to 'that').
segments.s607-g80: گذر کرد is a common compound expression meaning 'passed' (گذر 'passage' used with کرد as light verb). بر بوم ما marks motion across/over our homeland ('بر' + بوم 'land' + possessive ما 'our'). شهریار, placed after the verb phrase, is the subject in postverbal position — 'the king passed over our land.' Poetic word order often leaves the subject at the end.
segments.s607-g1: بیامد is a past third-person verb ('came/arrived') placed first in poetic word order. سوم روز is a time-expression 'the third day' that follows the verb. شبگیر here modifies روز (’the day of the night-hunt’ or 'night-hunt day'), and شاه ('the king') is the topic/subject placed after the time phrase for emphasis.
segments.s607-g2: سوی is a directional preposition 'toward'; دشت نخچیرگه is a nominal phrase 'the plain (dشت) of the hunting-ground (نخچیرگه)' with نخچیرگه functioning as a postnominal qualifier; با سپاه ('with an army') is an accompanying-agent phrase introduced by با ('with'). The whole segment is a directional/accompaniment clause attached to the verb.
segments.s607-g3: به + noun gives a locative 'at/to (the) ...'; به دست چپش = 'at his left hand/side' (چپ + enclitic -ش = 'his left'). هرمز is the personal name and کدخدای is a title in apposition ('the village headman'). The whole phrase marks a person standing on the king's left.
segments.s607-g4: سوی here is again directional 'toward' or 'at (the side of)'; راستش is راست + possessive -ش and means 'his right (side)' (note: the supplied sense list did not include this meaning, so the precise dictionary index is not available). موبد is the Zoroastrian priest title; پاک‑رای is an epithet 'of pure reputation/virtuous' attached to the priest.
segments.s607-g5: برو here functions as a discourse particle ('thereupon/then') rather than an imperative 'go' (the provided senses list only gives literal 'go', so the discourse use is not in the supplied senses). داستانها is the plural object 'stories/tales'. همی + خواندند is a classical-progressive construction: همی marks continuous or iterative aspect and خواندند is past plural 'they recited/read'.
segments.s607-g6: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of/about' introducing the source or subject of the speech (here 'about Jam and Fereydun'). جم و فریدون are the proper names joined by و. سخن راندند is an idiomatic verbal phrase: سخن 'words/speech' + راندند (literally 'they drove') used to mean 'they spoke/told', where راندند is the past plural of راندن.
segments.s607-g7: This is a list of hunting animals and their relative positions. سگ و یوز ('dogs and cheetah') are placed 'در پیش' = 'in front/leading', and شاهین و باز are two kinds of hawks/falcons accompanying them. The conjunctions و simply list the animals; باز here is the noun 'hawk/falcon' not the adverb 'again'.
segments.s607-g8: همی marks continuous action ('kept on'); تا is the subordinating 'until'; به سر برد is an idiom meaning 'to bring to an end / to spend or pass (time)' — سر here is 'head/end' and برد is the past of بردن used in that idiom. روز دراز is the object 'the long day'. (Note: the supplied senses for برد do not include this idiomatic 'spend/pass' meaning, so that specific sense is not available in the list.)
segments.s607-g129: چو آمد به هنگام خرم بهار is a temporal clause: چو + past verb آمد = “when ... came/arrived.” به هنگام is a fixed prepositional phrase meaning “at the time of,” followed by the adjective خرم modifying بهار. Word order is typical poetic Persian: temporal marker (چو) + verb (آمد) + time phrase (به هنگام خرم بهار).
segments.s607-g130: سوی دشت نخچیر شد شهریار has verb-before-subject order common in poetry: شد (past of شدن) plus the subject شهریار at the end. سوی دشت نخچیر = “toward the plain of game/hunting” (سوی = toward, دشت = plain, نخچیر = game/hunting-ground). شد here marks the action of moving/coming about (past).
segments.s607-g131: ابا موبدش نام او روزبه: ابا here is an archaic spelling/usage corresponding to modern با (“with”), introducing a companion. موبدش = موبد + possessive suffix -ش “his mobed/priest.” نام او روزبه is an appositive construction “his name (was) Rowzbeh,” with نام (name) followed by the pronoun او and the name.
segments.s607-g132: چو هر دو رسیدند نزدیک ده: چو + رسیدند forms a subordinate temporal clause “when they arrived.” هر دو = “both” (هر here = each/every used idiomatically with دو to mean both). رسیدند is past plural “they arrived,” and نزدیک is an adverb/adjective “near,” which governs the omitted noun (ده ‘village’) that follows in the line; Persian often omits the copula or places locality after the verb.
segments.s607-g133: نگه کرد فرخنده بهرام گور: نگه کرد = looked/turned a gaze (نگه 'gaze' + کرد as the light verb), with the verb preceding its subject, a common poetic order. فرخنده is an epithet/adjective “auspicious” modifying the following proper name; بهرام گور is the subject (Bahrām the Onager).
segments.s607-g134: جهان دید پر کشتمند و ستور: جهان دید = “(he) saw the world.” پر + (preposition) is the “full (of)” construction (پرِ + از ...), here followed directly by nouns; کشتمند and ستور enumerate what fills it. The clause omits any copula because Persian often uses verbless nominal predicates or compact predicates after a vision verb.
segments.s607-g135: برآورده زو کاخهای بلند: برآورده is a past participle/verb meaning “raised/produced” (here passive/intransitive sense ‘rose’ or ‘were raised’). زو is the poetic contracted form of از + او = “from him/of him,” marking source/agent. کاخهای بلند is the noun phrase “tall palaces” (کاخ + plural suffix + adjective بلند after the noun, a common poetic order).
segments.s607-g136: همه راغ و هامون پر از گوسفند: همه ... پر از = “all ... were full of.” Here the verb “were” (بود) is omitted, a normal nominal verbless construction in Persian. راغ و هامون are two landscape nouns joined by و, and گوسفند is the filler of the phrase “full of sheep.”
segments.s607-g169: بکوشید is a third‑person past (poetic) form of کوشیدن (“to strive/endeavor”) meaning “he strove.” The conjunction و links two coordinated clauses. ویرانی (literally “destruction/ruins”) is the direct object and آباد together with کرد forms a causative phrase: آباد کرد = “made (it) prosperous/inhabited.” Word order follows a common classical pattern: verb (بکوشید) … object + adjective + کرد (آباد کرد).
segments.s607-g170: دل زیردستان is a possessive phrase: دل (heart) with زیردستان (“the subjects/vassals”) specifying whose hearts. بدان here functions adverbially (‘with that/thereby’); the chunk is best read as بدان شاد کرد = “he made (them) glad by that.” کرد is the past‑tense causative “made.” Note the object (دل زیردستان) precedes the instrumental/adverbial (بدان) and the verb.
segments.s607-g171: چو introduces a temporal clause “when.” مهتر یکی (literally “chief one”) = “one became the chief” (یکی is an indefinite numeral/particle). گشت شد is a poetic double verbal expression meaning “became” (both verbs function to mark change of state). رای راست states the result: رای (“counsel/opinion”) + راست (“true/right”), i.e. “his counsel turned true.”
segments.s607-g172: بیفزود is a past third‑person form “he added/increased.” خوبی is the direct object (“goodness/virtue”). The conjunction و links the second object کژی (“crookedness/wrongness”), and بکاست is the verb meaning “he lessened/reduced” (ب‑ + کاست ‘reduced’) — together: “he increased good and lessened crookedness.”
segments.s607-g173: نهانی is an adverb “secretly.” بدیشان is a dative/enclitic form “to them.” نمودم is first‑person past “I showed/did show,” and بدی is the direct object “evil/harm” (i.e. ‘I secretly showed them the bad’). Word order places the adverb first, then indirect object, then verb and direct object.
segments.s607-g174: وزان پس = “and/from that then/after that” (a temporal connector). گشادم is first‑person past poetic form “I opened” (گشا‑ + ـدم). در here is the noun “door/gate” rather than only the preposition, and ایزدی is an adjective meaning “divine,” so در ایزدی = “the door/gate of the divine.” The whole means “and from that afterward I opened the divine door.”
segments.s607-g175: This is a verbless comparative clause: سخن بهتر از گوهر نامدار = “speech is better than a renowned gem.” بهتر از introduces the comparison (X better than Y); گوهر نامدار is the noun phrase compared (a famed gem).
segments.s607-g176: چو = “when.” بر جایگه is a locative phrase “upon/at its place/position” (بر + جایگه). بر برندش is a verbal phrase with an omitted plural subject “they”: برندش = بر + برند + ـش (object suffix) meaning “they put/carry it upon (it)” — the suffix ش refers back to سخن. به کار is an idiomatic resultative “into use / to work.” Together: “when they set it in its place, they make use of it.”
segments.s607-g145: بدو is the fused archaic form of به + او meaning 'to him' (indirect object). گفت موبد is verb (گذشته) + subject in inverted, so literal order 'to him said the mōbed' = 'the mōbed said to him'. که introduces a reported/clausal complement ('that'); از یک سخن is a source/agent phrase introduced by از ('from/by/with one utterance') modifying the following verb.
segments.s607-g146: این شارستان کهن is the noun phrase 'this ancient city' (این + noun + adjective). آمد is the past-tense verb 'came' (here used in sense 'came into being/was brought into being'); به پای literally 'to/into the foot/base' is idiomatic here as part of the predicate with آمد meaning 'came into existence' or 'was achieved' — word order is subject (این شارستان کهن) after the verb-complement cluster 'به پای آمد' because of poetic inversion.
segments.s607-g147: همان functions anaphorically here ('that same' / 'that very [place]') referring back to the city; از یک اندیشه is an ablative phrase 'from one thought/plan' giving the cause or instrument. آباد شد is the perfective/passive-like construction 'was made prosperous' (آباد شدن = 'to become populated/prosperous') — subject (همان) is understood as the thing that became آباد.
segments.s607-g148: دل شاه ایران is a possessive/genitive noun phrase 'the heart of the King of Iran' (noun + title + place-name). ازین (از + این, here orthographically joined as 'ازین') marks cause ('from/ because of this'). شاد شد is past 'became glad' (شد as the verbal auxiliary 'became'); word order places the result clause after the causal prepositional phrase for emphasis.
segments.s607-g149: مرا here is the accusative 'me' (written as م + را in the segmentation); شاه is the subject 'the king' and فرمود is the past tense verb 'he commanded/said'. کاین is the fused form of که + این ('that this' / 'that') introducing the content of the command. سبز جای is the noun phrase 'green place' (سبز adjectival). Word order is topical: object 'مرا' appears before the verb, and the subordinate clause follows فرمود.
segments.s607-g150: This subordinate clause expresses the commanded action: به دینار (instrumental/measure phrase 'in/with dinars') + گنج (treasure) is the object; اندر آور is 'bring into' where اندر (in/within) precedes the verbal stem آور (bring) — together functioning as the verb of the subordinate clause. The final به پای is idiomatic/adverbial, used here to mean 'at once'/'without delay' (poetic phrasing). The whole clause is the content of the king's command directed at 'me'.
segments.s607-g151: بترسیدم is first-person past 'I feared/dreaded' (classical Persian past form). از introduces the object/marker of fear 'from/of' and links to the following nominal phrase کردگار جهان ('the Creator of the world'), where کردگار governs جهان as a genitive ('Creator of the world').
segments.s607-g152: نکوهیدن is a deverbal noun 'to be reproached/being blamed' or 'reproach'; از here marks the source/agent of reproach ('from/by'). کهتران و مهان is a paired contrast 'the low (inferiors) and the great (magnates)' — an idiomatic 'low and high' expression. The whole phrase expresses fear of being censured by people both humble and high.
segments.s607-g33: دگر (here) works as a discourse adverb meaning 'then/again' and begins the turn; گفت is the past tense verb 'said' with موبد as its subject. بدان مردمان is a prepositional phrase 'to those people' — بدَان here is an archaic fusion of به + آن (to/that), with مردمان 'people' as the object of the preposition.
segments.s607-g34: که introduces the reported clause ('that' / introduces what was said). جاوید is an adjective 'eternal', دارید is present 2nd‑person plural 'you have' used here to express possession/wish in the reported sentence, and دل شادمان is the noun phrase 'joyful hearts' (دل = heart, شادمان = joyful/happy). The clause is a blessing/wish reported by the preceding گفت.
segments.s607-g35: شما را marks 'you' with the definite object/topic marker را; همه یکسره is an adverbial phrase 'all entirely/wholly'. کرد is the past verb 'made/did'. مه here functions as the subject in the classical/poetic order after the verb (an archaic or dialectal form corresponding to 'I'), so the clause reads 'I made you all entirely.'
segments.s607-g36: بدان is an adverbial pronoun 'by that/with that' (derived from به + آن). تا introduces purpose ('so that / in order that'), کند is the present/subjunctive form 'may do / might make', شهره is 'renown/fame' used transitively here as 'to make famous', and این خوب is the noun phrase 'this good (one)' — with the understood noun 'ده' (village) following in the full line. The whole segment expresses purpose: 'so that (he/I) may make this good village famous.'
segments.s607-g37: بدین ده is a locative expression 'in this village/place' (بدین = 'in this' or 'by this' in archaic/poetic spelling). زن و کودکان 'women and children' is the coordinated subject, and مهترند is a present plural predicate meaning 'are greater/more important' or 'are to be esteemed first.' The clause states a local social rule about rank or respect.
segments.s607-g38: کسی را uses کسی in the sense 'no one' (topic marked by را). نباید is impersonal modal 'must not / ought not', and که introduces the subordinate clause; فرمان برند is a verbal phrase 'to assume/take command' (برند is subjunctive/infinitival sense of بردن here meaning 'to take/assume'). Together: 'No one should assume command.'
segments.s607-g39: بدین ده again sets the location 'in this village'. The correlative 'چه ... و چه ...' functions as 'whether ... or ...' or 'both ... and ...', listing مزدور 'mercenary' and کدخدای 'village headman' as two types of persons being referred to.
segments.s607-g40: به یک راه is an adverbial phrase 'in one way / equally / the same way'. باید is the impersonal/modal 'must / ought', and که دارد/دارند جای is a subordinate clause 'that they have a place/standing' (دارند 'they have', جای 'place/position/rank'). The whole means they should hold the same rank or be treated equally.
segments.s607-g65: دل شاه بهرام is a possessive phrase without the spoken ezâfe — literally 'the heart of King Bahram.' ناشاد گشت uses گشت (past of گشتن/شدن) as 'became' so the clause means 'the heart ... became unhappy/sorrowful.' Word order is noun + possessor (poetry omits the ezâfe).
segments.s607-g66: ز یزدان بترسید is causative/relative: ز (=از) + یزدان marks the cause/object 'from/because of God' and بترسید is simple past 'he feared.' و links the next clause. پر داد گشت is a poetic/periphrastic expression: پر here functions adverbially/qualitatively 'full (of)' and داد گشت (داد + گشت) works like 'became/gave way to' — overall 'he feared God and became filled (with fear/dread).' گشت again is the past 'became.'
segments.s607-g67: به موبد چنین گفت is 'he said thus to the mobed' (به + indirect object). گفت here is the reporting verb 'said' (the provided senses list did not include the verb sense). کای روزبه is a vocative: کای = 'O' addressing روزبه by name.
segments.s607-g68: دریغست is an exclamation 'it is a pity/alas.' ویران is the noun/adjective 'ruined/desolate' that follows; چنین خوب ده contains an imperative (ده 'give/make') — the speaker is commanding restoration: 'make (it) so good/beautiful.' In spite of the terseness of the line, the sequence reads as lament + command to restore the ruin.
segments.s607-g69: برو is an imperative 'go!' Tیز here modifies the command ('swift') and آباد گردان is a compound imperative: آباد (prosperous) + گردان (imperative of گرداندن 'make/turn') = 'make prosperous.' به گنج gives the direction/target 'to the treasury/treasure-house.' Note گردان is used as a verb form (imperative) though the provided senses list showed nominal senses.
segments.s607-g70: چنان کن is an imperative 'do so/act thus.' کزین پس is a temporal phrase 'from this on / henceforth' (کزین = 'from this,' پس = 'after/then'). نبینند is third person plural present/subjunctive used here for future/optative 'they shall not see/experience' and رنج is the object 'suffering/hardship.' Together: 'Do it so that henceforth they see no suffering.'
segments.s607-g71: ز پیش شهنشاه is a poetic locative/ablative 'from before the shah' meaning 'having left the shah's presence.' موبد برفت uses برفت (an older/poetic past) meaning 'he departed/went away' — برفت here is the past verb 'left.'
segments.s607-g72: از آنجا به ویران is a directional phrase 'from there to the ruin.' خرامید is a past tense verb 'he strode/pranced proudly' (characteristic poetic motion). The final token تفت does not fit the usual lexical senses given and likely is either a scribal/metrical element or a name; it is not acting with the culinary senses listed, so its sense is unspecified here.
segments.s607-g153: بدیدم is the simple past first-person 'I saw' (ب- prefix + past stem). چو here = 'when' introducing a temporal subordinate clause. یک دل 'one heart' is the grammatical subject; دو اندیشه کرد is a small predicate where اندیشه (thought) combines with the light verb کرد ('did/made') — literally 'the one heart made two thoughts', i.e. 'the heart was of two minds.'
segments.s607-g154: ز هر دو = 'from/between both (of them)' (ز = 'from', هر دو = 'both'); برآورد is the past of برآوردن used here as a verb meaning 'took/removed/chose' (the heart acting on the two thoughts); ناگاه is an adverb 'suddenly'; گرد is a verbal/archaic form related to گردیدن 'to turn', so the clause means 'it suddenly turned away from both.'
segments.s607-g155: همان چون is a classical formula meaning 'just as' or 'in the same way that' (همان = 'just/that', چو = 'as/like'). به یک شهر = 'in one city' (به here = 'in/at'); دو کدخدای follows the noun and gives the quantity+title: 'two village-heads/ chiefs.' Word order mirrors classical Persian: preposition + noun + qualifiers.
segments.s607-g156: بود is past 'there was/were' (here past copula). بوم ایشان = 'their abode/land' (بوم + possessive pronoun ایشان). نماند is past negative of ماندن 'did not remain / was not left'; به جای is an idiomatic unit 'in place' — together 'their land did not remain in place' (i.e. their settlement did not last/intact).
segments.s607-g157: برفتم is an archaic/past verbal form meaning 'I went / I rose' (ب- + past stem = 'I went up/away'); بگفتم is 'I said' (ب- + past). به introduces the indirect object 'to': به پیران ده = 'to the village elders' (here پیران = 'elders', ده = 'village' attached as a genitive modifier, so 'the elders of the village').
segments.s607-g158: که‌ای here is the vocative/exclamatory 'که ای' = 'O!' addressing the addressees. مهتران = 'chiefs/elders' (archaic plural of مهتر). بر شما نیست = 'is not upon/over you' → idiomatically 'there is not ... among you'; مه is a poetic/archaic noun here meaning 'man/head/authority' (i.e. 'there is no man/real head among you'). The copula نیست is present negative used in reported speech.
segments.s607-g159: زنان is the plural subject 'women'. کدخدایند is the present/past-stative form 'are village-heads' (کدخدای + اند, the copulative ending). و = 'and'. کودک همان = 'the child likewise' (همان used adverbially/pointing back: 'that same/indeed the child').
segments.s607-g160: پرستار = 'nurse / caretaker' and is here a predicate applied to کودک (the child is the caretaker). و = 'and'. مزدورتان = مزدور + possessive suffix تان 'your mercenary/hired man' (shows the child's role as a paid/servile helper); این زمان = temporal phrase 'at this time / now' modifying the whole clause.
segments.s607-g97: ز is the preposition 'from' introducing a source. گنج جهاندار is a possessive phrase 'the treasure of the sovereign' where Ferdowsi omits the ezâfe; the head گنج (treasure) is followed by its possessor جهاندار (the ruler). دینار is the object (dinar/coin) and خواه here is an archaic exhortative/imperative (‘want/take’) addressed to the addressee.
segments.s607-g98: هم 'also' adds another source; از is the preposition 'from'. A series of nouns (تخم و گاو و خر و بار) is joined by و 'and' without ezâfe; this enumerates kinds of property (تخم 'seed', گاو 'cattle', خر 'donkey', بار 'load/goods'). The final خواه repeats the exhortation/imperative 'take/wish' and applies to the listed items.
segments.s607-g99: بکش is a direct imperative 'kill!'. هرک is the archaic fused form of هر که 'whoever'. The participial/adjectival بیکار 'idle' modifies that indefinite, and بینی 'you see' is a second-person present used to form a relative clause 'whoever you see (to be) idle'. به ده here indicates location ('in the village' — ده = village) or the place where the finding occurs; the verb-first order (imperative + relative clause) is typical of Persian commands.
segments.s607-g100: همه 'all' is the subject; کهترانند is a present-tense predicate 'they are inferior' (3rd-person plural). یکسر is an adverb 'entirely/wholly' modifying the predicate. تو is the vocative/subject 'you', and مه here is a poetic honorific (not the literal 'moon' or 'mist') meaning 'mighty/Great One' — a common epithet in Ferdowsi’s style.
segments.s607-g101: بدان here functions as a fused prepositional phrase 'to/at that' (ب- + آن) introducing the indirect object 'that mobed' (موبد). پیش is the locative/prepositional adverb 'before/in the presence of', نفرین 'a curse' is the noun object, and مکن is the negative imperative 'do not do' (prohibitive). The clause orders the prohibition toward the addressee: 'Do not (مکن) curse (نفرین) that mobed (بدان موبد) in his presence (پیش)'.
segments.s607-g102: نه is a clause-level negator 'not'. Word order is somewhat inverted: بر آرزو 'against (or upon) hope/desire' marks the domain affected, راند is the past tense verb 'he drove/caused to flee' (here meaning 'to frustrate' or 'to put to flight'), او is the 3rd-person pronoun 'he', and این سخن 'these/this words' names the utterance in question. Together the clause denies that the man spoke those words to destroy hope.
segments.s607-g103: اگر 'if' marks a conditional clause. یار is the object 'a companion/fellow' (here 'companion'). خواهی is the second-person present 'you want/wish' (main verb of the condition). ز درگاه شاه is a prepositional phrase 'from the court of the king' (ز = 'from', درگاه = 'royal court', شاه = 'king'); note Ferdowsi’s omission of an ezâfe between درگاه and شاه is poetic.
segments.s607-g104: فرستمت is a verb with enclitic object '-ت' meaning 'I will send you' (contextually future/intent); چندانک 'to that extent/so much' sets the limit. خواهی is again 'you desire/wish' and بخواه is an imperative 'ask!' — together forming the promise: 'I will send you as much as you wish; ask for it.' The sequence shows an offer followed by an imperative prompting the addressee to state desires.
segments.s607-g161: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning “when.” مهتر شدند is a past (perfective) verb phrase: مهتر “chief/elder” + شدند (3rd pl. past of شدن) “became/were made.” آنک بودند که is a relative/demonstrative clause “those who were,” where آنک = “those/that (who),” بودند is past “they were,” and که introduces the following clause; the whole segment sets a temporal/subject frame (“when those who were chiefs became …”).
segments.s607-g162: This clause describes the event: به خاک اندر آمد is an old-fashioned word order meaning “came/ fell into the dust/earth” (به + خاک + اندر + آمد, with اندر intensifying the locative). سر مرد مه is the noun phrase ‘the head of the man/hero (مرد) described as مه “great”’; سر is literal “head.” The verb آمد governs the whole locative phrase.
segments.s607-g163: به گفتار is an instrumental/ablative phrase “by/through word/report” (به used adverbially = “by”). ویران شد is a passive/resultative phrase: ویران “ruined” + شد (past) “became/was made.” این پاک جای is the noun phrase “this pure place” (این = “this,” پاک = “pure/clean,” جای = “place”). Word order is topical (instrumental first) typical of classical Persian.
segments.s607-g164: This short clause states an ethical stance: نکوهش ز من دور = “reproach/blame is distant from me” (نکوهش = “reproach,” ز = poetic ‘from,’ من = “me,” دور = “far”). و ترس از خدای = “and fear of God” (ترس = “fear,” از = “of/from,” خدای = “God”); the copula is omitted as is common in Persian, so the meaning is roughly ‘may reproach be far from me, and (may) fear of God (be present).’
segments.s607-g165: ازان پس is a temporal phrase “after that/thereupon” (ازان = “from that,” پس = “after/then”). بریشان is the older fusion of بر + ایشان meaning “to/for them” (direction/beneficiary). ببخشود (past, 3rd sg.) + شاه gives the finite clause ‘the king pardoned/granted them’ — ببخشود here is a past-tense verb meaning ‘forgave/forgave them/relieved them.’
segments.s607-g166: نمودم دگرگونه راه is ‘I showed a different way/path.’ نمودم is 1st person past of نمودن “to show,” دگرگونه “different,” and راه “way/path.” The initial برفتم is a 1st-person past verb meaning ‘I departed/ went forth’ (archaic/compound form of رفتن with a prefix); its lemma senses given in the data appear to be for ‘snow’ and do not fit here, so this token is an archaic verb form meaning ‘I went away.’
segments.s607-g167: یکی با خرد پیر کردم = “I made/appointed one/ someone old with wisdom.” یکی is an indefinite “one/someone,” با خرد = “with wisdom” (instrumental), پیر = “old/elder” (here used as a respectful ‘elder/old man’), and کردم is 1st person past of کردن “I made/appointed.” به پای literally “to the foot/of the side” functions idiomatically as ‘at the side of / in attendance to.’
segments.s607-g168: This is a coordinate list of roles: سخن‌گوی “speaker/spokesman,” بادانش is a fused form (با + دانش) meaning “learned / with knowledge” (i.e. ‘a learned one’), and رهنمای “guide.” The conjunction و links the items; the whole phrase specifies the functions the appointed elder will perform.

sentences.s607-s93: They bestowed upon him the kingly robe, and they raised his head aloft among the clouds.
sentences.s607-s53: When the old man heard these words he became glad and was freed from his long‑standing sorrow.
sentences.s607-s54: At that moment the old man turned toward the house, (and) he brought the people to the pond.
sentences.s607-s55: He set about making the land prosperous, and he began entrusting all the frontiers.
sentences.s607-s56: They asked the neighbours for cows and asses, and they thoroughly made the whole plain beautiful.
sentences.s607-s5: When the shining sun reached the dome, he saw no sign of a grave or a gazelle anywhere.
sentences.s607-s6: When the shining sun became a maker of coins, he returned from the hunting-ground, heavy of heart.
sentences.s607-s7: A certain green-placed figure came forward, and within it were many people and beasts.
sentences.s607-s8: From that place many came onto the road; spectators appeared before the army.
sentences.s607-s29: The plain had become a ruin and the dwellings lay desolate; people and four‑footed animals had fled from it.
sentences.s607-s30: When a year had passed and spring arrived, the king went that way to hunt.
sentences.s607-s31: He reached that prosperous, cheerful place; he looked around and saw no one there nor in the village.
sentences.s607-s32: All the trees were dry and every dwelling was ruined; the whole frontier was without people or beasts.
sentences.s607-s69: All desire was water and every plain a stream; every village was full of fair‑faced people.
sentences.s607-s70: Lambs were scattered over his mountains and plains; his land became entirely heavenly.
sentences.s607-s71: He said to the mobad thus: 'O Rouzbeh, what have you done that has made this good village ruined?'
sentences.s607-s72: People and four‑footed beasts were scattered away from him; what did you give that caused them to make the place prosperous?
sentences.s607-s57: They themselves and the frontier-guards strove hard, and they planted several trees wherever (they could).
sentences.s607-s58: When one plain became prosperous, the heart of whoever saw it there became glad.
sentences.s607-s59: From that place, whoever fled had blood pouring down on (his) eyelashes.
sentences.s607-s60: When news came from the prosperous place, also about the suffering of this old village‑headman.
sentences.s607-s13: May it be the lair of foes, snares, and game; may the water inside the stream be like pitch.
sentences.s607-s14: The mobed knew what the king's command was, and from there he set off toward the village by the road.
sentences.s607-s15: He said to them thus: 'This green place is full of houses, people, and quadrupeds.'
sentences.s607-s16: The king of kings Bahram welcomed him; he renewed this desire.
sentences.s607-s9: The sovereign was wrathful and impetuous; he wished to contrive a plot to descend upon that village.
sentences.s607-s10: No one among them applauded; you said, "Tie those donkeys to the ground."
sentences.s607-s11: Because of those people the king became narrow‑hearted; he did not treat them kindly.
sentences.s607-s12: He said to the mobed thus: "This green place is full of houses, people, and beasts of burden."
sentences.s607-s61: One by one they turned their faces toward the village; in every public square they dug/raised a stream and made it flourish.
sentences.s607-s62: Those very birds, cows, asses, and sheep each increased (in number) one by one.
sentences.s607-s63: Wherever anyone planted a tree, that ruined place became like a lush paradise.
sentences.s607-s64: In three more years the village was further adorned; from labor all desire of the 'mah' was fulfilled.
sentences.s607-s89: The king's wisdom should be the champion's speech, if you wish the soul to remain free from suffering.
sentences.s607-s90: May the king's heart be glad forever; may (it) be prospered, free from crookedness and ruin.
sentences.s607-s91: When the king heard these words, he said (exclaiming), "You are deserving of a crown, O Rouzbeh."
sentences.s607-s92: He bestowed a single golden dinar like the full moon; to that cultured/skillful man — the discerning hero — he (gave it).
sentences.s607-s45: Everything here is full of gloom; it has come to such a pass that we must weep.
sentences.s607-s46: Hearing those words, Ruzbeh was filled with sorrow; he asked and said, 'Which of you is the great one?'
sentences.s607-s47: He answered thus: the chief was in the place where the seeds of plants lay.
sentences.s607-s48: To him Ruzbeh said, 'You be the chief; preside over this ruined place.'
sentences.s607-s25: They all mingled with one another; blood was poured everywhere off the road.
sentences.s607-s26: When an uprising rose from that village, they suddenly took to flight from that place.
sentences.s607-s27: The elders remained (behind), blue-footed and full; the instruments of exercise and of the lute (so to speak) became ready.
sentences.s607-s28: They brought the whole village to ruin; the trees became dry and the stream ran without water.
sentences.s607-s21: Hand over every woman, child and man — all of them — one by one to the village headman.
sentences.s607-s22: A shout arose from the bountiful village, from their joy, for they had become continually glad.
sentences.s607-s23: After that the women and men became of one mind in counsel — the caretaker and the mercenary together with the village headman.
sentences.s607-s24: When the young man became depraved in the village, they suddenly cut off the man's head.
sentences.s607-s41: A foolish mobad came — one of those renowned, childless evildoers.
sentences.s607-s42: He said to us outright, 'You are all the greatest; be careful not to judge one person by another.'
sentences.s607-s43: He said this and that, and then it became tumultuous — filled with plunder, killing, and clubs.
